content:
You don’t need thirty AI apps — Cursor, ChatGPT or Claude, and Perplexity cover most of what I actually use in a week as a robotics engineer.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Allan Rodrigues on UnsplashI downloaded eleven AI tools in one weekend last year because a thread said I’d fall behind without them.
I used two twice and forgot the rest existed. The problem wasn’t that AI failed. I treated tools like collectibles instead of instruments. Depth on three beats breadth on thirty — that took me embarrassing months to accept.
Why three is enough (for now)
Tools don’t create leverage. Habits do.
Three well-used tools cover:

• Code navigation and editing — where files live and what they do.

• Thinking, debugging, explaining — turning confusion into next steps.

• Sourced lookup — docs and libraries without pure hallucination roulette.
Everything else is optional until a specific job proves otherwise.
Tool 1: Cursor (AI-native code editor)
What it is: A code editor built around AI assistance — chat with your codebase, ask about functions, generate edits in context.
When I open it: Unfamiliar repos. Personal projects. Open-source libraries I need to understand fast. Any time I’d otherwise scroll files randomly for an hour.
What it replaced for me: Half my blind grep sessions and “where does this even live?” panic.
What it didn’t replace: My judgment. I still read diffs. I still test. Explore before edit — that rule from M32 exists because Cursor can confidently change the wrong file.
If you’re a student: Use it on a small personal project or an open-source repo. Ask it to explain structure before asking it to fix bugs.
Tool 2: ChatGPT or Claude (pick one, learn both later)
What they are: General reasoning partners — debug help, concept explanations, script drafts, “explain this error like I’m tired.”
When I open them: After work problem-solving. Learning dense topics. Drafting automation scripts. Writing that needs clarity.
My split: Claude for long documents and careful explanations. ChatGPT for quick back-and-forth and brainstorming. Overlap is huge. Clear prompting matters more than brand loyalty (M33 goes deeper).
If you’re a student: Use one daily for two weeks on real coursework or project friction — not toy prompts.
Tool 3: Perplexity (sourced answers)
What it is: Search-meets-AI — answers with citations you can click and verify. In my experience as of 2026, citation quality varies by query; it is a strong starting point, not a substitute for primary sources.
When I open it: New libraries. ROS packages I haven’t used. “What changed in this API?” questions. Anything where I want a link to official docs, not just a confident paragraph.
What it replaced: Opening twelve browser tabs and losing the thread.
Honest limit: Still verify primary sources for anything graded or production-adjacent. It’s a filter, not a textbook.
What I deliberately don’t rotate daily
Image generators for engineering work. Ten different coding copilots. Every new launch thread.
Novelty feels productive. It’s usually distraction.
I try new tools on Sundays occasionally — one experiment, one verdict: daily driver, sometimes tool, or delete.
How to run the one-week depth experiment
Pick one tool from the list you barely use.
Days 1–7: use it only on real tasks — assignment code, lab report, project bug, doc you don’t understand.
Log:

• What worked?

• What wasted time?

• What would I do manually next time?
After a week, you’ll know more than any comparison article gave you.
Common student mistakes I made
Tool hopping before skill building — same bad prompt, different logo.
Skipping verification — AI-assisted plagiarism of wrong answers.
Ignoring fundamentals — tools amplify understanding or amplify confusion. Same multiplier.
Paying for pro tiers before free tiers hit limits — upgrade when friction is measured, not when marketing says so.
The stack is not the skill
Cursor won’t save you if you can’t read code. ChatGPT won’t save you if you can’t state the problem. Perplexity won’t save you if you never click the sources.
Tools are leverage on top of engineering habits — defining problems, testing, reading docs, asking clear questions.
What I don’t pay for yet (and why)
I upgrade when free tiers block real work — not when marketing emails panic me.
Cursor pro made sense when I hit usage walls on actual project weeks. Same logic for chat subscriptions: pay when friction is measured in hours lost, not vibes.
Students especially: master free tiers first. Tool FOMO is a tax on attention.
How these three connect in one evening
Typical after-work learning session:

• Perplexity — “what’s the standard approach to X?”

• ChatGPT or Claude — “explain this doc section and draft a minimal example.”

• Cursor — implement in a repo, ask about structure when stuck.
Triangle, not chaos. Each tool has a seat at the table.
What I removed from my dock
Every app I hadn’t opened in thirty days got archived or deleted.
Psychic weight of unused tools is real. Three icons you trust beat twelve you avoid.
Teaching a friend without overwhelming them
When classmates ask “what AI should I use,” I don’t send a list of twenty. I send this trio and one week of homework: real task, daily use, one note of what worked.
Depth spreads person to person slower than viral lists — but it sticks.
The takeaway
My working trio: Cursor + ChatGPT or Claude + Perplexity.
Not sponsored. Not complete for every specialty. Enough for most weeks in my robotics engineering life.
Pick one you underuse. Run it daily for seven days on real work. Depth first. Add tools when friction proves you need them — not when Twitter says you’re behind.
Which of the three do you already use — and which have you been ignoring?

If this helped, follow me on Medium — I publish daily on AI and engineering for people figuring it out like me.
